title: Share of Caribbean reefs with Acropora corals present or dominant
subtitle: The share of coral reefs in the Caribbean where major reef-building group of corals, _Acropora_, was dominant or present. The declines show the loss of coral cover as a result of pressures including water pollution, disease outbreaks, and [coral bleaching](#dod:coral_bleaching).
caption: Between 1950 and 2007, the share of Caribbean reefs with Acropora corals present or dominant shows a sustained decline across both metrics. The orange line, representing reefs where Acropora was present, begins at roughly 90% in 1950 and falls to approximately 20% by 2007. The blue line, tracking reefs where Acropora was dominant, starts near 80% and drops to below 10% over the same period. Both lines show a temporary recovery in the 1970s, with the "present" metric rising to roughly 75% and the "dominant" metric reaching 60%, before resuming a downward trajectory. From 1980 onward, the decline accelerates, with the "dominant" line falling below 20% around 1990. By the end of the timeline, the gap between the two metrics narrows, with both values remaining below 20%. The chart shows a consistent reduction in the prevalence of Acropora corals across the Caribbean region over the second half of the 20th century.
